coredata.dc:title: Development of a novel fluorescent aptasensor based on the interaction between hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of patulin
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Qi
Li, Zhijuan
Liu, Ningxin
Zhou, Yuting
Zhang, Fuyan
Li, Shuang
Jin, Peng
Xiang, Rui
Le, Tao
coredata.dc:description: There is an urgent need to develop an economical and convenient method for the ultrasensitive detection of patulin (PAT), a mycotoxin that can potentially harm human health when it is found in fruits and their derivatives. In this study, we have developed a novel fluorescent aptasensor that utilizes nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the fluorescent donor and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates as the fluorescent acceptor. N-CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method, resulting in spherical particles with a diameter of 7.6 nm. These nanoparticles exhibited excellent water solubility and displayed a vibrant blue emission at 448 nm when excited at 360 nm. Cobalt hydroxide nanoplates with a beta crystal structure [β-Co(OH)2] were synthesized using a simple co-precipitation method, exhibiting hexagonal plate-like shapes with uniform lateral sizes of 4–5 μm. The fluorescence of N-CDs can be efficiently quenched by hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates through Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism. The maximum quenching-recovery capability can be achieved when the concentrations of N-CDs-Apt and β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates are 150 nmol/L and 100 μg/mL, respectively. The pH of the TE buffer should be 8.0, and the incubation time should be 10 min at 25 °C. The developed fluorescent aptasensor displayed an excellent selectivity for PAT determination with a detection limit of 0.57 pg/mL in the linear range of 1.25 pg/mL-100 ng/mL. The rapid PAT determination in fruit juice samples was realized with good recoveries (96.9–105.8%). The developed fluorescent aptasensor based on the interaction between N-CDs and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates can be a promising method for the rapid and ultrasensitive detection of PAT in agricultural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Patulin
Fluorescent aptasensor
Hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Ultrasensitive detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of the β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and the aptamer coupled N-CDs
Fabrication and detection principle of the fluorescent aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the as-synthesized β-Co(OH)2: (A–C) Scanning electron micrograph images of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates with rosy red color (inset) and hexagonal structure (within the yellow dashed line) at various magnifications. (D) Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and (E) high-resolution TEM images (HRTEM) of hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates. (F) X-ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern of the formulated β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Characterizations of the as-prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs): (A) Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) image and particle size diagram of N-CDs with 0.22 nm lattice spacing (inset). (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs after excitation between 300 and 360 nm. (C) UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. The inset shows the N-CDs aqueous solution under daylight and ultraviolet light. (D) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under different storage time under daylight. (E) UV–Vis absorption spectra of patulin aptamer, N-CDs and their complex. (F) Gel electrophoresis image of N-CDs-labeled aptamer (lane 1) and the pure patulin aptamer (lane 2).

(A) Fluorescence spectra analysis of different samples, including N-CDs-Apt (black curve), N-CDs-Apt+β-Co(OH)2 (red curve) and N-CDs-Apt+β-Co(OH)2+Patulin (purple curve). (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and fluorescence emission spectrum of the N-CDs-Apt complex. Parameter optimization involves (C) Buffer types, (D and E) concentrations of N-CDs-Apt and β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, and (F) Incubation time of N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and patulin. Different lowercase letters represent significant differences, and the same lowercase letters represent no significant differences (p < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs-Apt complex with the addition of various concentrations of patulin (from a to h: 0, 0.00125, 0.0125, 0.125, 1, 10, 25, and 50 ng/mL). (B) The linear plot of fluorescence recovery efficiency as a function of logarithmic patulin concentration. (C) Selectivity evaluation of the aptasensor based on N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates for patulin (PAT, 5 ng/mL), ochratoxin A (OTA, 50 ng/mL), zearalenone (ZEN, 50 ng/mL), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1, 50 ng/mL), deoxynivalenol (DON, 50 ng/mL) and their mixture. Different lowercase letters represent significant differences, and the same lowercase letters represent no significant differences (p < 0.05).

(A) Nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified with patulin aptamer (denoted as N-CDs-Apt) via condensation reaction. (B) Schematic diagram of fluorescent aptasensing for ultrasensitive of PAT through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between N-CDs-Apt and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates.
Comparison of the fabricated assay with other aptasensing methods for PAT detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: MB-methylene blue; GCE-glassy carbon electrode; MWCNTs-multiwall carbon nanotubes; N-CDs-nitrogen-doped carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of patulin in apple juice samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Development of a novel fluorescent aptasensor based on the interaction between hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of patulin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Chongqing Normal University, No.37 Chengzhong Road, Shapingba District, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Chongqing Normal University, No.37 Chengzhong Road, Shapingba District, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is an urgent need to develop an economical and convenient method for the ultrasensitive detection of patulin (PAT), a mycotoxin that can potentially harm human health when it is found in fruits and their derivatives. In this study, we have developed a novel fluorescent aptasensor that utilizes nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the fluorescent donor and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates as the fluorescent acceptor. N-CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method, resulting in spherical particles with a diameter of 7.6 nm. These nanoparticles exhibited excellent water solubility and displayed a vibrant blue emission at 448 nm when excited at 360 nm. Cobalt hydroxide nanoplates with a beta crystal structure [β-Co(OH)2] were synthesized using a simple co-precipitation method, exhibiting hexagonal plate-like shapes with uniform lateral sizes of 4–5 μm. The fluorescence of N-CDs can be efficiently quenched by hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates through Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism. The maximum quenching-recovery capability can be achieved when the concentrations of N-CDs-Apt and β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates are 150 nmol/L and 100 μg/mL, respectively. The pH of the TE buffer should be 8.0, and the incubation time should be 10 min at 25 °C. The developed fluorescent aptasensor displayed an excellent selectivity for PAT determination with a detection limit of 0.57 pg/mL in the linear range of 1.25 pg/mL-100 ng/mL. The rapid PAT determination in fruit juice samples was realized with good recoveries (96.9–105.8%). The developed fluorescent aptasensor based on the interaction between N-CDs and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates can be a promising method for the rapid and ultrasensitive detection of PAT in agricultural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the well-known mycotoxins, patulin (PAT) is primarily produced by Penicillium, Aspergillus, and Byssochylamys. It is ubiquitously found in fruits and their products, posing serious health risks to consumers [1]. PAT can cause acute and chronic poisoning effects such as convulsions, edema, genotoxicity and immunotoxicity [2]. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has classified it as a category three carcinogen. Public concerns should be raised over the food safety issue of agricultural products contaminated by PAT. The Codex Alimentarius and organizations like the US Food and Drug Administration have allowed a maximum limit of 50 μg/kg for PAT in food [3]. However, the European Union has set a PAT level of 10 μg/kg in baby food [4]. Consequently, there is a growing global concern for PAT detection. Current conventional techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and gas chromatography) for PAT targeting still have many shortcomings, such as expensive equipment, complex operations and time-consuming procedures [5,6]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop economical, simple and sensitive alternative methods for PAT quantification.

Aptamers are a class of single-stranded DNA or RNA that can specifically bind to target molecules, exhibiting a high affinity through shape complementarity [7]. They are usually obtained through the screening process of the Ligand Evolution System by Exponential Enrichment (SELEX) [8], offering serveral advantages over antibodies, such as lower immunogenicity, smaller size, and ease of modification. These outstanding properties concerning aptamers facilitate them to construct biosensing platforms [9,10]. Among various analytical strategies, fluorescent aptasensors are the most pervasive and promising due to their simplicity, rapid response, and high sensitivity [11]. The fluorescent donor-acceptor pairs have been extensively adopted for the development of fluorescent aptasensors. Commonly used fluorescent probes include fluorescein, rhodamine, and other organic dyes, while graphene oxide (GO) [12], gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [13], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [14,15] and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [16] are commonly employed as nano-quenchers. Despite the successful utilization of dye-ssDNA/nano-quencher systems in fluorescent sensing, it is essential to acknowledge certain limitations associated with these materials, including the difficulty in preparation, high cost, and potential harmful to the environment [17].

In recent years, several novel fluorescent aptasensors have been successfully developed and applied in food analysis [18,19]. For example, researchers have developed an effective turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe containing upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and metal organic frameworks [MIL-101(Cr)] for the sensitive detection of T-2 toxin [20]. Compared with other fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as ideal candidates for fluorescence detection due to their excellent photoluminescence and photostability, good biocompatibility, low cost, and easy preparation and functionalization [21]. Recently, a CQDs-based fluorescent sensing platform utilizing resonance energy transfer was developed for aflatoxin B1 quantification [22]. Therefore, the development of aptasensors based on novel fluorescent donor-acceptor pairs can provide an excellent analytical platform for PAT targeting in food and agro-food products.

Cobalt hydroxide-based materials are highly regarded for their affordability, ease of preparation, high stability, excellent electron transfer rate, and impressive optical absorption. Therefore, they have been recognized as promising candidates for quenching applications in recent years [23]. Numerous studies have investigated the potential of cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets as novel nano-quenchers for fluorescent detection of biomarker molecules and enzyme activities [24,25]. However, few studies have used cobalt hydroxide [Co(OH)2] nanomaterials as the fluorescent donor to construct aptasensor. Herein, we successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates as novel fluorescent donor-acceptor pairs to construct aptasensor for the ultrasensitive detection of PAT. This study demonstrated a convenient and cost-effective fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates for sensitive and rapid PAT detection through Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate [Co(NO3)2·6H2O], aqueous ammonia solution (25%), methanol, sodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O), and urea were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Patulin (PAT), ochratoxin A (OTA), zearalenone (ZEN), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), and deoxynivalenol were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The functionalized PAT aptamer (3′–NH2–TTCCATTTTATAGTCACATCTACTACGCCCAACCGCCCGG-5′) [26] was synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-EDTA buffer solution (TE buffer) and 2-hydroxyethyl-piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid (C8H18N2O4S, HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and deionized Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ/cm) was used throughout the experiment for reagent preparation. Apple samples were purchased from local supermarkets in Chongqing, China.

The surface morphology and microstructure properties of the as-synthesized β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates were examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM-6510) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F), respectively. The crystal structures of the obtained samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffractometry (XRD) with Cu Kα irradiation using a Bruker (KAPPA Apex II). In addition, the functional groups were analyzed using Fourier transmission infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, EQUINOX 55, Germany).

Hexagonal-shaped β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates were synthesized by co-precipitation of cobalt nitrate solution with ammonia. Briefly, 10 mL of ammonia solution was gradually added dropwise to a 0.1 M cobalt nitrate solution accompanied under continuous heating and stirring at 80 °C. It can be observed that the blue precipitate in the reaction system transformed into a rosy red precipitate, indicating the phase transition from α-Co(OH)2 to β-Co(OH)2. Subsequently, the obtained precipitate was washed several times using methanol, centrifuged at 8, 357 g for 10 min, and dried under vacuum conditions at 60 °C for 8 h to obtain β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates.

Firstly, 1.970 g of sodium citrate and 2.413 g of urea were finely ground into a white powder and poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The hydrothermal reactants were heated at 180 °C for 2 h and dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water. To remove any insoluble impurities, centrifugation was performed at 9, 286 g for 10 min. The resulting upper solution was dialyzed for 24 h using cutoff dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 100 Da and then lyophilized using a freeze dryer. Next, the resulting N-CDs solution (1600 μL, 5 mg/mL) was combined with various buffer solutions and incubated in the dark for 2 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, 1600 μL of amino-functionalized PAT aptamer solution (1 μM) was added into the above mixed solution which was protected from light for an additional 2.5 h. The aptamer functionalized N-CDs, referred to N-CDs-Apt, were obtained by amide condensation after dialyzing for 24 h of dialysis.

The experiments for the fluorescent aptasensing of PAT were conducted with a fixed final volume of 250 μL. First of all, a certain amount of PAT was mixed with the optimal concentration of N-CDs-Apt and incubated at 25 °C for the recommended incubation time. Subsequently, different suspensions of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates were added to the aforementioned solution and mixed for 10 min at 25 °C. The final concentrations of PAT ranged from 0.0125 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL. The changes in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) were determined based on the formula: ΔF
F–F 0, where F and F 0 represented the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplate suspensions in the presence and absence of PAT, respectively.

Several potential mycotoxins existing in fruit juices including ochratoxin A (OTA), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), deoxynivalenol (DON) and zearalenone (ZEN) were used to verify the specificity of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor. Different amounts of interfering analytes was separately incubated with N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoprobe pairs under the optimized conditions, and their respective fluorescence intensities were measured.

The feasibility of the developed fluorescent aptasensor based on N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates was assessed using homemade apple juice. Juice samples were treated as previously described [27]. For the spiked recovery experiment, a given amount of PAT was spiked into as-prepared juice samples, which were subjected to the above-mentioned fluorescent determination. Finally, HPLC analysis, a standard method for detecting PAT concentrations in raw apple juice, was used to validate the results.

The experiments were conducted in triplicate. Data were presented as means ± standard deviations. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was adopted for the comparison among groups, followed by Duncan's multiple test (p < 0.05). Statistical analysis was carried out using SPSS version 22.0 (SPSS Inc., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and the aptamer coupled N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized β-Co(OH)2 powder exhibited a rosy red color. The structural characteristics of the powder were initially investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1 A. Results showed that the obtained β-Co(OH)2 displayed nearly perfect hexagonal plate-like shapes with homogenous lateral size of 4–5 μm and uniform thicknesses of 45–60 nm (Fig. 1B). Different from the previously reported smooth surface of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates [28], the surface of the as-prepared β-Co(OH)2 in this study exhibited numerous regular lines of bumps (Fig. 1C). Consistent with the SEM results, TEM observation also confirmed the presence of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates in a regular hexagonal shape (Fig. 1D). A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1E) clearly exhibits the clear lattice fringes of (010) and (110) crystal planes. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the hexagonal-shaped β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates is shown in Fig. 1F. All the observed diffraction peaks were fully indexed to the brucite-like hexagonal symmetry (JCPDS: 74-1057). Additionally, the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S1) the hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates at a concentration of 100 μg/mL exhibited a broad absorption centered at 495 nm, indicating its potential for spectral overlap with different fluorescent donors.

The morphological analysis revealed that the N-CDs prepared in this study presented a highly spherical structure (Fig. 2 A) with a uniform size of 7.6 nm (inset of Fig. 2A). The HRTEM image further showed that the measured adjacent lattice fringe to be 0.22 nm corresponding well to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene (inset of Fig. 2A). FT-IR spectra exhibited a broad peak in the range of 3421-3230 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H bond stretching vibrations (Fig. S2). A sharp peak at 1592 cm−1 is assigned to the CC stretching vibration of carboxylic acids. The characteristic peak at 1466 cm−1 can be attributed to a C–NH vibration. The broad peak at 500-1000 cm−1 was associated with the C–O stretching vibration. In the case of N-CDs, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups could result in good dispersibility and satisfactory stability in aqueous solutions [29].

The inset of Fig. 2C shows that the aqueous solution of N-CDs appears to be brownish-yellow under daylight and turns blue color when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. In addition, the corresponding photoluminescent emission wavelength of the N-CDs remains consistent regardless of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2B). The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the prepared N-CDs were determined to be 360 nm and 448 nm (Fig. 2C), respectively. The UV–Vis absorption peak observed at 331 nm may be attributed to the presence of π-π* conjugate structures within the aromatic sp2 domains of the N-CDs [30]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared N-CDs demonstrated good stability, with only a 6.7% loss in fluorescence intensity after 30 days (Fig. 2D). After PAT aptamer modification, the obtained N-CDs-Apt complex not only retained the characteristic absorption peak of N-CDs but also exhibited a distinct aptamer absorption peak at 257 nm (Fig. 2E). Gel-electrophoretic analysis further demonstrated the significantly slower movement of the PAT aptamer modified N-CDs (N-CDs-Apt) complex (lane 1) compared to the pure PAT aptamer (lane 2) due to the mass increase of the aptamer after binding to N-CDs (Fig. 2F). These results collectively indicate the successful preparation of the N-CDs-Apt complex in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and detection principle of the fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the surface of the water-soluble N-CDs was rich in carboxyl groups, allowing for the conjugation of NH2-modified PAT aptamer through a condensation reaction (Scheme 1 A). Based on the specific binding between the aptamer and its target molecule, a fluorescent aptasensor for PAT was fabricated at room temperature (Scheme 1B). In the absence of hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, the N-CDs-Apt complex exhibited a strong emission band when excited at 360 nm (the black curve in Fig. 3 A). However, upon the addition of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs-Apt complex significantly decreased (red curve in Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows a considerable spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of N-CDs (the donor fluorophore) and the absorption spectrum of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates (the acceptor fluorophore). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime became shorter after the addition of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates (1.33 ns) compared to that of the N-CDs-Apt complex alone (3.97 ns), and it then returned to 2.01 ns after adding PAT to the reaction system. The alteration of the fluorescence lifetime indicated the presence of dynamic quenching.

Accordingly, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates can be realized through the FRET mechanism. On the other hand, the specific binding of the aptamer to PAT caused the detachment of the N-CDs-Apt complex from the β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, resulting in a change in the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system that was positively correlated with the concentration of PAT.

To achieve a high detection sensitivity, various aptasensing parameters, including buffer types, concentration of the N-CDs-Apt complex and β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, and the incubation time of the aptamer and target PAT, were optimized in this study. Since the choice of buffer type could significantly influence the detection signal of the developed fluorescent aptasensor, TE buffer was selected as the optimal buffer for subsequent experiments in this study (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity changes (ΔF) of the N-CDs-Apt complex exhibited a gradual rise, reaching a peak at 150 nM of the N-CDs-Apt complex before sharply declining (Fig. 3D). Additionally, concentrations of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates (10–400 μg/mL) initially increased and then decreased. The highest quenching-recovery capability was achieved with a concentration of 100 μg/mL of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates (Fig. 3E), which could be attributed to the limited solubility of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates at higher concentrations. Moreover, the fluorescence change values of the aptasensing system reached a plateau within a short duration of 10 min after incubation at 25 °C with 250 rpm oscillation (Fig. 3F). Based on the above findings, it could be inferred that the optimal aptasensing system was consisted of TE buffer (pH 8.0), 150 nM of the N-CDs-Apt complex, 100 μg/mL of β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates and an incubation time of 10 min at 25 °C.

The proposed method has outstanding selectivity and minimal interference from potential interferers such as OTA, ZEN, AFB1, and DON (Fig. 4C), owing to the high affinity between the aptamer and its target. Besides, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of ten measurements of PAT was less than 8.4%, demonstrating that the aptasensing assay exhibited a good repeatability. The mean spiked recoveries of the fabricated aptasensor ranged from 96.9% to 105.8%, with coefficients of variations (CVs) between 2.53% and 8.83% (Table 2 ). The correlation coefficient between the test results and the standard HPLC method reached 0.9946. Accordingly, the proposed fluorescent aptasensor based on N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates can be used for detecting PAT in real samples as well.

In summary, a novel fluorescent aptasensor was developed for the ultrasensitive detection of PAT in fruit juice. The aptasensing platform utilized N-CDs as the fluorescent donor and hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates as the fluorescent acceptor. The fluorescence quenching of N-CDs-labeled aptamer by hexagonal β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates is achieved through Förster resonance energy transfer. When the aptamer specifically binds to PAT, it induces the separation of the N-CDs-Apt complex from the β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence. Under optimal detection conditions, the fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs-Apt/β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates had a good linear range of 1.25 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 0.57 pg/mL. The aptasensing platform developed in this study exhibited a rapid ability to detect the concentration of PAT in real samples with satisfactory recoveries (96.9–105.8%). Therefore, this study demonstrated the effectiveness of a stable and cost-efficient strategy for detecting PAT with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Qi Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing. Zhijuan Li: Investigation, Visualization, Data curation, Software. Ningxin Liu: Investigation, Validation, Software. Yuting Zhou: Data curation, Software. Fuyan Zhang: Investigation. Shuang Li: Software. Peng Jin: Data curation. Rui Xiang: Writing – review & editing. Tao Le: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission, China (KJQN202100516).

Front. Plant Sci.

Toxins
